title: Quiz: Recursive QAOA
description: Test your understanding of Recursive QAOA, correlation measurements, and variable elimination techniques

questions.question: What is the primary motivation for using Recursive QAOA instead of standard QAOA?

options.text: To reduce the number of variational parameters
To incrementally reduce problem size using correlation information
To avoid quantum circuits entirely
To increase the circuit depth

questions.explanation: RQAOA uses correlation measurements from QAOA outputs to identify strongly correlated variable pairs. It then eliminates variables iteratively, reducing the effective problem size while preserving solution quality.

questions.question: In RQAOA, what does a strong positive correlation $\langle Z_i Z_j \rangle \approx +1$ indicate about the optimal solution?

options.text: Variables $i$ and $j$ should have opposite values
Variables $i$ and $j$ should have the same value
Variable $i$ should always be 0
The variables are independent

questions.explanation: A positive ZZ correlation close to +1 means the variables tend to have the same value (both |0⟩ or both |1⟩). In MaxCut terms, these vertices are likely in the same partition in good solutions.

questions.question: When eliminating variable $j$ with 'opposite' relation to variable $i$, what happens to an edge $(j, k)$?

options.text: It is simply removed from the graph
It becomes an edge $(i, k)$ with the same weight
It becomes an edge $(i, k)$ with negated weight
It is converted to a self-loop on $k$

questions.explanation: When $j$ is opposite to $i$, we substitute $s_j = -s_i$. An edge $(j, k)$ contributes $s_j \cdot s_k = -s_i \cdot s_k$, effectively negating the edge weight when remapping to $(i, k)$.

questions.question: In RQAOA, the cutoff parameter determines when to stop recursing and solve the remaining problem exactly.

options.text: True
False

questions.explanation: True. The cutoff (typically 3-5 variables) is the size at which RQAOA stops recursively eliminating variables and instead solves the remaining small problem exactly through brute force enumeration.

questions.question: What is the purpose of backtracking in RQAOA?

options.text: To undo incorrect eliminations
To reconstruct the full solution from the reduced solution
To optimize the QAOA parameters
To reduce memory usage

questions.explanation: After solving the reduced problem exactly, backtracking reconstructs the full solution by reversing through the elimination history. Each eliminated variable's value is determined by its reference variable and the recorded relation (same/opposite).

questions.question: What is a 'connected correlation' in the context of RQAOA analysis?

options.text: The correlation between connected components
The covariance: $\langle Z_i Z_j \rangle - \langle Z_i \rangle \langle Z_j \rangle$
The correlation along a path in the graph
The sum of all pairwise correlations

questions.explanation: Connected correlation (covariance) removes the product of marginal expectations from the raw ZZ correlation. This reveals genuine statistical dependencies beyond what individual variable biases would predict.

questions.question: Which of the following are advantages of RQAOA over standard QAOA? (Select all that apply)

options.text: Can achieve better approximation ratios on some problem instances
Reduces the effective problem size at each iteration
Always guarantees the optimal solution
Can exploit problem structure revealed by correlations

questions.explanation: a) RQAOA often achieves better ratios especially on structured problems. b) Each elimination reduces the problem size. c) Incorrect - RQAOA is still a heuristic and doesn't guarantee optimality. d) Correlation information reveals problem structure that guides variable elimination.

questions.question: When eliminating the edge between the eliminated variable and its reference variable, what constant is added to the objective for 'opposite' relation?

options.text: 1 (the edge is always cut)
The edge weight divided by 2

questions.explanation: When two variables are in opposite partitions, the edge between them is always cut (contributes to the cut value). This contribution is a constant (+1 for unweighted graphs) that must be tracked during reduction.
